---
title: "Session 8: AI Tools III"
subtitle: "Code-native agents (Claude Code) at research scale"
---

::: {.callout-note}
## This session is under development
The detailed walkthrough will be added before **May 4, 2026**. The outline below mirrors the planned 75-minute structure. This session closes the running exercise started in [Session 6](session6.qmd) and continued in [Session 7](session7.qmd).
:::

## Claude Code: a code-native agent (~15 min) {#claude-code-intro}

- The third mode: an agent that lives in your terminal and works directly against a project directory.
- What changes from Cowork: the repository is the primary interface. The agent reads and writes files, runs commands, and commits through git, all inside a single working tree.
- Why this matters for research. The reproducible artifact is the repo itself: code, `renv.lock`, commits, and a log of what the agent did.
- Mode B is enforced by design here. The session is ephemeral. The code and the commits remain.
- Setup check: everyone has Claude Code installed from the Session 7 homework and can reach a project folder with git initialized.

## Scaffolding a multi-department scraper (~25 min) {#scaffold}

- Target: five department placement pages, selected from the pre-vetted list used in Sessions 6 and 7.
- Goal output: one tidy `placements_all.csv` with columns `department`, `name`, `year`, `placement`, `source_url`.
- Live demo: one prompt to scaffold the project (`R/` folder, one scraper per department, a shared `combine.R`, a top-level `run_all.R`).
- Students mirror the scaffold on their own laptops. Each commits after the scaffold is in place.
- What to watch for: the agent inventing a department that is not in the list, silently changing the CSV schema between scrapers, dropping `source_url`.

## Classification: rule-based and LLM-based (~20 min) {#classification}

- The new column: `placement_type` with values `academic`, `non_academic`, `postdoc`, `unknown`.
- First pass: a rule-based classifier in R based on keyword matching ("Assistant Professor", "Postdoctoral", "Federal Reserve", etc.). Students read the rules and can defend each one.
- Second pass: an LLM classifier for the rows the rules leave as `unknown`. One API call per row, cached to disk.
- Disclosure practice: the prompt, the model, and the date are recorded in the script. The cache file is committed. A reviewer can rerun and get the same numbers.
- Verification: hand-label twenty random rows and compare.

## Analysis and descriptive write-up (~10 min) {#analysis}

- Simple tabulations: counts by `department` and `placement_type`; share academic by year; ten most common institutions.
- One figure: academic share by department, with a clear caveat about sample size and page coverage.
- One paragraph of text, written by the student, summarizing what the data show. Not written by the agent.

## Synthesis and course wrap (~5 min) {#synthesis}

- The three-modes table, now filled in with what each mode cost and what it delivered.
- One rule that carries forward: keep the code, discard the session. Mode B wins because it is the only mode that survives peer review.
- Final commit, final push, final tag. The repo is the record.
- Course feedback and next steps for independent practice.
